Dynamics and stability of the keyhole in full-field power-modulated laser welding unveiled by in-situ X-ray imaging
Chunkai Li a,b,*, Zhongbo Zhao a, Yu Shi a,b, Yu Pan a

a State Key Laboratory of Advanced Processing and Recycling Non-ferrous Metals, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou, 730050, PR China b Key Laboratory of Non-ferrous Metal Alloys and Processing Ministry of Education, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou, 730050, PR China
H I G H L I G H T S

• Revealed keyhole dynamics during full-field power-modulated galvanometer laser welding using in-situ microfocus real-time X-ray imaging. • Key geometric features of the keyhole were extracted to quantitatively characterize its dynamic stability. • Mechanisms of circular, infinity, and 8-shaped trajectories affecting keyhole stability and porosity suppression were elucidated. • An energy density optimization for laser superposition regions improved keyhole stability and eliminated porosity.
A R T I C L E I N F O
A B S T R A C T
Keywords: Full-field power-modulated laser Weld pool Keyhole In-situ X-ray imaging

Full-field power-modulated laser welding is an emerging laser welding technology. It stabilizes the dynamic behavior of the keyhole by precisely regulating the spatiotemporal distribution of laser power. As a result, welding defects such as porosity and cracking are effectively suppressed. However, the large number of controllable process parameters, the difficulty in visualizing the keyhole, and the unclear mechanisms by which process parameters influence keyhole dynamics result in a lack of guidance in process optimization during practical welding applications. To address this issue, an in situ micro-focus X-ray real-time imaging system was used to directly monitor the dynamic behavior of the keyhole during laser welding of titanium alloy under different scanning trajectories and power modulation modes. Characteristic parameters, including depth vari­ ance, opening size, and depth-to-width ratio, were extracted. These parameters were combined with numerical simulation to analyze the effects of scanning trajectory and power modulation strategy on keyhole stability. The results indicate that, compared with conventional laser welding, the overall stability of the keyhole is signifi­ cantly enhanced under circular, infinity, and 8-shaped scanning trajectory modes, accompanied by a pronounced reduction in weld porosity. However, a small number of pores are still observed in regions where the laser trajectories superposition .This phenomenon is attributed to a sudden increase in keyhole depth within the trajectory superposition regions, which leads to reduced stability and even keyhole collapse. By adopting a control strategy that lowers the energy density in the laser trajectory superposition regions, the dynamic stability of the keyhole can be further improved, which effectively suppresses welding porosity.
1. Introduction

Beam shaping is an emerging laser welding technology. It enables the modulation of the laser beam amplitude, phase, polarization state, and spot geometry through optical systems. As a result, a conventional Gaussian beam can be transformed into a customized beam that is tailored to meet diverse and complex welding requirements [1,2]. In the

field of laser welding, typical beam shaping strategies include oscillation laser welding, annular beam configurations, and full-field power- modulated lasers [3–5]. Such customized beam profiles demonstrate significant potential for overcoming technological bottlenecks in laser welding, effectively addressing critical issues such as excessive heat- affected zones and limited process windows. Consequently, beam shaping provides essential technical support for achieving high-quality,

C. Li et al. Journal of Manufacturing Processes 166 (2026) 135–151
high-efficiency, and highly stable laser welding [6,7].

Full-field power-modulated laser welding, as a beam shaping tech­ nique, achieves active stabilization of molten pool–keyhole dynamics through precise control of laser power in both temporal and spatial domains [8,9]. At the macroscopic scale, this technique effectively suppresses welding defects such as spatter and porosity, resulting in a significant enhancement of welding process stability. At the microscopic scale, this technique facilitates the optimization of alloying element distribution and weld microstructural morphology, which leads to improved comprehensive mechanical properties of the joints [10,11]. Compared with conventional laser welding, full-field power-modulated lasers exhibit distinct advantages in weld quality control and micro­ structural property regulation. They have been widely applied in various manufacturing sectors, including automotive manufacturing, aerospace, rail transportation, and high-end equipment production, demonstrating strong potential for engineering use [12].

labor and material resources. In addition, numerical simulation enables visualization of key welding phenomena, such as temperature gradients, molten pool flow, and keyhole motion [14]. Li et al. [15] investigated keyhole dynamics and pore formation mechanisms in adjustable annular-mode laser welding of medium-thickness aluminum alloys. This investigation combined experimental measurements with numerical simulations. Through this combined approach, the fundamental mech­ anism by which annular lasers suppress porosity was elucidated. Liu et al. [16] employed numerical simulations to analyze the dynamic behavior of the keyhole during sinusoidal-trajectory laser welding of 7075 aluminum alloy and proposed an optimization strategy that re­ duces porosity defects by decreasing the laser dwell time in unstable regions. This strategy was further validated experimentally, achieving a reduction in porosity to below 1%. Han et al. [17] conducted numerical simulations to examine molten pool flow and keyhole dynamics in 6061 aluminum alloy welding under full-field power modulation modes. The results demonstrated that, compared with conventional constant power modes, power-modulated modes significantly increased the molten pool flow velocity and the keyhole opening size. This increase contributed to an enhancement in keyhole stability. However, the coupling of multi­ physics models in numerical simulations faces significant challenges in terms of convergence and computational cost. The computational pro­ cess typically relies on idealized assumptions and is highly sensitive to boundary conditions. Although such models can intuitively capture the physical phenomena occurring during the welding process, effective validation methods are still lacking at present, and their accuracy largely depends on indirect measures such as weld morphology and porosity distribution [14].

Compared with conventional laser welding, full-field power-modu­ lated laser welding enables more flexible control of energy input, providing a novel technical pathway for stabilizing keyhole dynamics. However, it also significantly increases the complexity of the welding process [13]. This technique introduces multiple adjustable process parameters, including scanning trajectory, scanning frequency, oscilla­ tion amplitude, and energy modulation modes, resulting in strongly coupled and highly nonlinear process characteristics. Meanwhile, the dynamic evolution of the keyhole interior is difficult to observe directly and in real time, and the underlying mechanisms governing keyhole dynamics and their stabilization remain unclear. Under these circum­ stances, current process parameter optimization relies heavily on empirical trial-and-error approaches. These approaches are labor- intensive and lack clear guidance. As a result, the engineering effi­ ciency and industrial adoption of this technology are constrained when compared with conventional laser welding. Therefore, systematically elucidating keyhole dynamic behavior and its response to power mod­ ulation parameters constitutes a critical scientific issue for achieving stable welding control and rational optimization of process parameters.

To elucidate the dynamic evolution mechanisms of the keyhole during laser welding and their influence on welding stability, re­ searchers have employed a combination of numerical simulations and in situ monitoring techniques. These methods are used to systematically analyze the intrinsic relationship between the dynamic stability of the molten pool-keyhole system and pore formation. Numerical simulation offers numerous advantages in the study of oscillation laser welding and laser–arc hybrid welding. These advantages include process parameter optimization, reduction of trial-and-error costs, and conservation of

To investigate keyhole dynamic behavior, researchers have employed various approaches to explore the underlying physical mechanisms. These approaches include high-speed imaging observa­ tions of welding glass and sandwich structures, as well as X-ray obser­ vations during the welding process. However, owing to the substantial differences in physical properties between glass and metallic materials, as well as the discrepancies between sandwich structures and actual welding configurations, the applicability of these observation methods is inherently limited [18]. In situ X-ray real-time imaging, as an emerging in situ observation technique, enables direct visualization of keyhole dynamic behavior within the molten pool by transmitting X-rays through metallic materials [19]. Yuan et al. [20] employed in situ high- speed synchrotron X-ray imaging to directly observe, for the first time, the dynamic processes by which annular beams suppress porosity, molten pool fluctuations, and spatter during laser powder bed fusion (LPBF). Liu et al. [21] likewise utilized in situ microfocus X-ray imaging
C. Li et al. Journal of Manufacturing Processes 166 (2026) 135–151

stability. To address this challenge, full-field power-modulated laser welding was combined with an in situ X-ray real-time imaging system. The keyhole dynamic behavior under full-field power modulation was directly observed. Key characteristic parameters of the keyhole were quantified and combined with numerical simulation. The effects of different scanning trajectories and power modulation modes on keyhole dynamic behavior and stability were systematically analyzed. This study aims to reveal the physical mechanisms of keyhole stability in full-field power-modulated laser welding. It also aims to establish the relationship between keyhole dynamic behavior and welding defect formation. In addition, it provides experimental evidence and physical guidance for the optimization of multi-parameter welding processes based on full- field power-modulated lasers.

2.2. Materials and process parameters
0.5 10 Infinity trajectory mode 8-shaped trajectory mode

TC4 titanium alloy was used as the base material in this study. The specimen size was 100 mm × 8 mm × 4 mm. Its chemical composition is listed in Table 1. Before welding, the surface oxide layer was removed with sandpaper. The surface was then cleaned with absolute ethanol to remove dust and oil. A suspension composed of anhydrous copper sul­ fate and tungsten powder was uniformly coated on the sample surface. The diameter of the tungsten particles was 0.3 mm. A density difference exists between tungsten particles and TC4 titanium alloy. Clear molten pool contours can be obtained under X-ray irradiation. In addition, anhydrous copper sulfate fixed the tungsten particles after drying and prevented them from being blown away by the shielding gas. During the experiment, the sample was placed in a shielding chamber filled with argon to prevent oxidation of the titanium alloy. The argon flow rate was 10 L/min. The chamber was made of transparent acrylic plates. Slots were designed on the top of the chamber to ensure X-ray transmission along the paths of the laser beam and the X-ray beam.

in LPBF to successfully observe the powder melting behavior of 316 L stainless steel at different layer thicknesses, quantitatively character­ izing spatter size, ejection velocity, and ejection angle, and revealing the role of spatter in melt track formation under high layer thickness con­ ditions. Wu et al. [22] addressed the tendency of conventional linear scanning strategies in LPBF to induce defects such as porosity and keyhole instability. They employed synchrotron-based in situ X-ray imaging to elucidate the dynamic evolution mechanisms of the keyhole in Ti-6Al-4 V alloy under kHz-level oscillating laser beams. This inves­ tigation clarified the regulatory effects of oscillating laser beams on keyhole stability, porosity suppression, and melt track surface morphology. At present, in situ X-ray real-time imaging techniques have been primarily applied to conventional laser welding and LPBF pro­ cesses. In contrast, systematic in situ experimental investigations into keyhole dynamic behavior—particularly the evolution of keyhole sta­ bility—during full-field power-modulated laser welding remain scarce.
Based on the current research status and existing limitations, advanced in situ monitoring methods are required to further investigate the relationship between keyhole dynamic behavior and keyhole

For full-field power-modulated laser welding, the primary process parameters include scanning trajectory, power distribution mode, oscillation amplitude, and scanning frequency. Excessively high scan­ ning frequencies and amplitudes can alter the thermal interaction mode of the laser welding process, leading to a transition from the keyhole mode to the heat conduction mode, in which no keyhole is formed within the molten pool [23]. Therefore, the present study first focuses on the effects of full-field power modulation strategies on keyhole dynamic behavior during laser welding. Circular, infinity-shaped, and 8-shape
C. Li et al. Journal of Manufacturing Processes 166 (2026) 135–151
scanning trajectories were employed, while the oscillation amplitude, scanning frequency, and welding speed were kept constant. The detailed process parameters are summarized in Table 2.

With the introduction of power modulation, the number of experi­ mental parameters increases dramatically, and relying solely on orthogonal experimental designs would result in an excessive experi­ mental workload and a high degree of uncertainty. Therefore, this study adopts a combined approach of laser energy field calculation and X-ray real-time imaging. On the basis of clarifying the influence of scanning trajectories on keyhole dynamic behavior, the process parameters for the power modulation modes were formulated. The detailed parameters are provided in Section 3.4.

displayed the real-time position of the laser spot to confirm its actual location. After an additional delay of 100 ms, the worktable motion system was started at T3. Welding was then performed for 10 s, after which the laser was turned off at T4. After a further delay of 100 ms, the worktable motion system was stopped at T5. The shutter was closed at T6, and finally, the X-ray source was turned off at T7.In this experi­ mental setup, a control strategy in which the laser is activated prior to the initiation of worktable motion was adopted. This is because full-field power-modulated lasers exhibit an unstable phase during startup. To ensure reliable real-time imaging, the laser was switched on 100 ms before the worktable motion began. This procedure ensured the for­ mation of the molten pool and allowed the molten pool depth to meet the imaging requirements.
2.3. X-ray real-time imaging control
2.4. Image processing

During laser welding, laser energy is absorbed by the plate, and a molten pool forms once the energy reaches the melting point of the base material. As the energy further increases to the vaporization threshold,
C. Li et al. Journal of Manufacturing Processes 166 (2026) 135–151
Circular trajectory mode: { x(t) = x0+Vwt + Asin(2πft + φx) y(t) = y0 + Acos ( 2πft + φy
Infinity trajectory mode: { x(t) = x0+Vwt + Acos(2πft + φx) y(t) = y0 −0.5Asin ( 4πft + φy
8-shape trajectory mode: { x(t) = x0+Vwt + 0.5Asin(2πft + φx) y(t) = y0 + 0.5Acos ( πft + φy

liquid metal evaporates and metal vapor is expelled from the molten pool, leading to keyhole formation under the combined effects of recoil pressure, surface tension, thermal buoyancy, and gravity. At this stage, the solid base material, molten pool, and keyhole cavity coexist. After keyhole formation, the molten pool surface collapses, and vapor gen­ eration inside the keyhole creates a cavity that reduces the density of the molten pool, resulting in a density difference between the molten pool and the solid base material. According to the Beer–Lambert law, [24] the intensity of transmitted X-rays is inversely proportional to the local average density of the sample. This density variation leads to different projection intensities in different regions: X-rays passing through the keyhole cavity exhibit the highest intensity and appear brightest in the images, followed by those passing through the molten pool, while the lowest intensity corresponds to the solid base material. In this study, ImageJ software was used to process the original radiographic images. Grayscale calibration, sharpness enhancement, and contrast optimiza­ tion were performed. Molten pool boundary images were then gener­ ated. The boundaries of the keyhole and the molten pool were manually delineated. In laser welding, keyhole stability is a critical factor gov­ erning porosity formation. Previous studies have demonstrated that keyhole depth, opening size, and depth-to-width ratio are key parame­ ters determining keyhole stability [25–28]. Therefore, after processing the raw images, key parameters including keyhole depth, opening size, and depth-to-width ratio were further extracted to evaluate keyhole stability, as illustrated in Fig. 4.
2.5. Laser energy field calculation

In these equations, x₀ and y₀ denote the initial coordinates; V₍w₎ is the welding speed; A is the oscillation amplitude; f is the scanning fre­ quency; t represents time; and the phase difference satisfies φₓ − φᵧ = π/2. To directly evaluate the dispersion effect of trajectory modulation on laser energy, the transient line energy of the scanning path, defined as Epath, was introduced. This parameter represents the average line energy distributed along the total laser scanning path within a given time. It reflects the influence of trajectory expansion on energy homogenization and the stirring effect in the molten pool. A smaller Epath, indicates a stronger homogenization effect and a stronger stirring effect. Epath, can be calculated as the ratio of the total theoretical input energy to the total scanning path length within a given time. Its expression is given as follows:

To investigate the influence of full-field power-modulated laser welding on the surface energy distribution of the weld, MATLAB was employed to calculate the laser energy distribution. In this study, laser welding was used, and the heat source was modeled as a typical Gaussian heat source. At the focal position, the laser spot exhibits a Gaussian energy distribution. The energy equation proposed by Wang et al. [29], incorporating a modified thermal diffusion coefficient, was adopted and is expressed as follows:

3. Results and discussion
3.1. Characteristics of energy distribution

Gaussian; however, due to the higher superposition density at the weld center, the peak energy density increases to 710.22 J/mm2.In contrast, the 8-shape trajectory results in a markedly different energy distribu­ tion. In addition to periodicity, a pronounced symmetry is observed. From the side view, the distribution exhibits a center-low and side-high pattern, with energy concentrated away from the center. The peak en­ ergy densities on the two sides are 526.71 J/mm2 and 528.91 J/mm2, respectively. These differences mainly arise from the fact that, during welding, the laser spot moves along the welding direction while simul­ taneously undergoing periodic motion along the prescribed trajectory. The superposition of laser spot paths during this motion leads to energy superposition, which results in distinct energy distribution characteris­ tics for different trajectory modes.
3.2. Distribution characteristics of weld porosity

3.3. Evolution process of keyhole and porosity formation mechanism

different trajectory modes. It can be observed that the number of pores in the non-oscillation mode is significantly higher than that in the trajectory-modulated modes, with pores randomly distributed along the weld. Among the trajectory-modulated modes, the 8-shaped trajectory exhibits a higher porosity than the circular and infinity-shaped trajec­ tories, while the infinity-shaped trajectory results in the lowest porosity. Spatially, the pore distribution in the trajectory-modulated modes dif­ fers markedly from that in the non-oscillation mode. As indicated by the X-ray images, pores in the circular and infinity-shaped trajectories are mainly concentrated near the weld center, whereas those in the 8- shaped trajectory are predominantly distributed on both sides of the weld. Combined with the laser energy field distributions discussed in Section 3.1, these results indicate that, under trajectory modulation, pore locations correspond closely to regions of energy superposition

center along the welding direction for the circular trajectory, leading to energy superposition and a transient increase in keyhole depth at the superposition region. Fig. 8(c) shows the keyhole dynamics under the infinity trajectory mode. Similar to the circular trajectory, the keyhole depth follows a periodic “shallow–deep–shallow” pattern. When the laser spot reaches 225 ms and 300 ms, trajectory superposition at the weld center induces energy superposition, resulting in greater keyhole depths at these time points than at other moments within the cycle. Fig. 8(d) depicts the keyhole dynamics under the 8-shaped trajectory mode, which differs markedly from the circular and infinity-shaped modes. The keyhole depth is significantly larger at 200 ms, 250 ms, and 275 ms than at other times in the cycle. As indicated in Section 3.1, for the 8-shaped trajectory, energy superposition caused by trajectory superposition occurs on both sides of the weld, leading to transient

The other three trajectory modes show significantly improved stability compared with the non-oscillation mode. The infinite-shaped trajectory exhibits the highest stability. Fig. 9b and d show the variation of keyhole opening size under different trajectory modes. Fabbro et al. [30] re­ ported that an increased keyhole opening diameter leads to a more uniform distribution of recoil pressure induced by metal vapor, resulting in a more stable dynamic equilibrium of the keyhole and reducing porosity caused by keyhole collapse. Moreover, the keyhole opening

increases. The open area on the upper surface of the molten pool be­ comes larger. The molten pool flow becomes more stable and smoother. Bubbles can reach the molten pool surface more easily. The escape channel becomes less obstructed. As a result, the probability of bubble escape increases. According to the results, the keyhole opening size in the non-oscillation mode is significantly smaller than that in the other three trajectory modes. The infinity-shaped trajectory mode shows the largest keyhole opening diameter. The average keyhole depth and the
C. Li et al. Journal of Manufacturing Processes 166 (2026) 135–151

average keyhole opening size during welding were statistically analyzed. The depth-to-width ratio of the keyhole was then calculated. The results are shown in Fig. 9e. A smaller value indicates stronger geometric stability of the keyhole. When the depth-to-width ratio in­ creases, the keyhole tends to extend toward the deeper region of the molten pool. The actual travel distance of bubbles from their generation position to the molten pool surface increases. A larger depth-to-width ratio also leads to higher flow resistance in the lower region of the molten pool. The bubble motion becomes slower. Bubbles are more likely to be trapped and form pores. The results show that the non- oscillation mode has the poorest geometric stability. The other three trajectory modes perform better than the non-oscillation mode. Among them, the infinity-shaped trajectory mode shows the highest geometric stability. Based on the analysis of keyhole depth variance, keyhole opening size, and keyhole depth-to-width ratio, the keyhole stability can be ranked from high to low as follows: infinity-shaped trajectory mode, circular trajectory mode, 8-shaped trajectory mode, and non-oscillation mode.

welding direction. No energy superposition caused by trajectory overlap is formed. The laser energy is highly concentrated in a fixed region. This results in a sudden change in recoil pressure on the keyhole wall. The force balance of the keyhole is disturbed. The keyhole collapses and pores are formed. At the same time, the high concentration of energy significantly increases the keyhole depth-to-width ratio. This reduces the geometric stability of the keyhole. It also extends the escape path of bubbles. As a result, pores are more likely to form. Previous studies have shown that trajectory modulation can effectively homogenize laser en­ ergy in the molten pool region. As shown in Fig. 11b, under the trajec­ tory modulation mode, the energy distribution becomes more uniform. The fluctuation of recoil pressure is reduced. The keyhole does not fracture. The keyhole depth decreases. The keyhole opening width in­ creases. The depth-to-width ratio decreases. The keyhole stability is significantly improved. However, when the laser spot moves to the en­ ergy overlap region, as shown in Fig. 11c, local energy becomes instantaneously concentrated. On one hand, the keyhole depth increases sharply. On the other hand, the recoil pressure shows strong fluctua­ tions. A protrusion forms on the inner wall of the keyhole. The dynamic force balance of the keyhole is disturbed. The keyhole becomes unstable and collapses. Finally, pores are formed.
3.4. Porosity suppression strategy based on power modulation
Based on the results presented in Sections 3.1–3.3, scan trajectory modulation can significantly enhance keyhole stability; however, it
C. Li et al. Journal of Manufacturing Processes 166 (2026) 135–151
C. Li et al. Journal of Manufacturing Processes 166 (2026) 135–151

cannot entirely eliminate porosity formation in regions of trajectory superposition, where pore locations closely coincide with the super­ position zones. Accordingly, a porosity mitigation strategy based on power modulation is proposed. This strategy aims to reduce the degree of energy superposition at trajectory superposition regions. As a conse­ quence, keyhole stability is further improved. On this basis, corre­ sponding power modulation schemes were developed for the three trajectory modes, with the associated power modulation equations expressed as follows:
Circular and infinity trajectory mode:

modulation, a similar periodic pattern is observed, with the pre- modulation near-Gaussian distribution shifting to a center-low, side- high profile. The peak energy densities on the two sides are 723.24 J/ mm2 and 696.37 J/mm2, and the central energy density decreases to 691.45 J/mm2. For the 8-shaped trajectory with power modulation, the energy distribution retains its pre-modulation periodicity and symme­ try, while the peak energy densities on the sides decrease to 420.86 J/ mm2 and 419.96 J/mm2. Power modulation significantly alters the en­ ergy distribution characteristics in all three trajectory modes, effectively reducing the energy superposition caused by trajectory superposition. According to X-ray inspection results, porosity in the welds is completely suppressed under all three trajectory-modulated modes.
8-shaped trajectory mode:

Here, PM represents the modulated power, P is the laser power, A is the oscillation amplitude, 0.97 is the power scaling coefficient to protect the laser, and 0.30 is the power modulation gradient coefficient. Fig. 12 illustrates the power modulation for the three trajectory modes. In the circular and infinity trajectories, the power increases along the y-axis, with the minimum power at the weld center. In the 8-shaped trajectory, the power decreases along the y-axis toward the intersections of the trajectory with the y-axis. In all three modes, the minimum power is set to 70% of the original laser power.

Figs. 14a–14c show the keyhole dynamic behavior under different trajectory power modulation modes within one typical motion cycle. Under the power modulation mode, the energy distribution pattern changes. The energy superposition positions of the circular and infinity trajectories shift. The superposition location moves from the weld center to both sides of the weld. As a result, the keyhole depth variation changes from a “shallow–deep–shallow” pattern to a “deep­ –shallow–deep” pattern. For the 8-shaped trajectory mode, the keyhole depth at the energy superposition position is significantly reduced compared with the conventional power mode. Figs. 15a–15d show the keyhole depth variation, opening width, and depth-to-width ratio under different trajectory power modulation modes over multiple motion cy­ cles. Under the circular trajectory mode, the average keyhole depth decreases to 1.24 mm. The keyhole opening width increases to 1.42 mm. The depth-to-width ratio decreases to 0.87. Under the infinity trajectory mode, the average keyhole depth decreases to 1.29 mm. The keyhole opening width slightly decreases to 1.55 mm. The depth-to-width ratio decreases to 0.83. Under the 8-shaped trajectory mode, the average keyhole depth decreases to 1.50 mm. The keyhole opening width in­ creases to 1.44 mm. The depth-to-width ratio decreases to 1.04. Fig. 15b shows the variance of keyhole depth under different trajectory power
C. Li et al. Journal of Manufacturing Processes 166 (2026) 135–151

modulation modes. The results indicate that after introducing power modulation, the variance of keyhole depth decreases significantly for all trajectory modes. This result indicates a strong enhancement of keyhole stability. At the same time, the keyhole opening size increases signifi­ cantly compared with the conventional power mode. This condition is more favorable for pore escape. Fig. 16 shows the CFD simulation results of keyhole dynamic behavior in the energy superposition region under two power modes. Compared with the conventional power mode, the keyhole opening width is larger under the power modulation mode. The depth-to-width ratio is smaller. In addition, the inner wall of the keyhole is smooth and no protrusions are observed. The keyhole stability is significantly improved.
4. Conclusion

1. This study employed in-situ micro-focus X-ray real-time imaging to successfully observe the dynamic behavior of keyholes during laser welding of TC4 titanium alloy under various scan trajectories and power modulation modes. Keyhole stability was assessed using characteristic parameters such as keyhole depth, opening size, and depth-to-width ratio to analyze the effects of trajectory and power modulation strate­ gies. The experimental results show that the infinity-shaped trajectory yields the lowest number of pores, while the non-oscillation mode pro­ duces the highest porosity.

2. Compared with the conventional laser mode, trajectory modula­ tion significantly improves keyhole stability. The depth variance de­ creases from 0.23459 to 0.05206 (circular), 0.03454 (infinity), and 0.09339 (8-shaped). The opening size increases from 1.14 mm to 1.31 mm, 1.60 mm, and 1.39 mm, respectively. The depth-to-width ratio decreases from 1.35 to 1.09, 0.92, and 1.25. Overall stability improves markedly, and the number of weld pores decreases. The infinity trajec­ tory shows the highest stability. However, a small number of pores still appear in the trajectory superposition region.

3. Introducing power modulation on top of trajectory modulation markedly alters the laser energy distribution, reducing the energy con­ centration caused by superposition. As a result, the keyhole depth variance and depth-to-width ratio are further decreased, suppressing instability induced by energy superposition and successfully achieving complete elimination of weld porosity

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Experimental system.
Fig. 2. Schematic diagram of the power modulation principle (taking a circular path as an example).

Fig. 1 illustrates the experimental setup, which consists of a full-field power-modulated laser welding system and an in situ keyhole moni­ toring system. The welding system employs a full-field power-modu­ lated hybrid manufacturing platform independently developed by Lanzhou University of Technology. This system not only enables mod­ ulation of scanning trajectories but also allows arbitrary adjustment of laser power at 36 phase points along the scanning path. Through this capability, coordinated spatiotemporal modulation of laser power is achieved. A schematic illustration of the power modulation principle is shown in Fig. 2. The in-situ keyhole monitoring system comprises several key components. A microfocus X-ray source (Comet FXE225.48) was used to generate the X-ray beam, with a maximum tube voltage of 225 kV and a maximum tube current of 1.5 mA. Through preliminary calibration, optimal imaging quality was obtained at a tube voltage of 220 kV and a tube current of 1.4 mA. The X-ray beam was oriented perpendicular to the welding direction, and a side-view perspective of the welding process was achieved through the relative motion between the worktable and the imaging system. In addition, the system was equipped with a CANON E5759HD-P1K X-ray image intensifier with a spatial resolution of 30 μm and a dynamic acquisition rate of 2000 fps.
Fig. 3. Schematic diagram of X-ray real-time imaging control.
Fig. 4. Schematic diagram of image processing.

Fig. 3 illustrates the control scheme of the X-ray real-time imaging system. The X-ray source was activated at time T0. After clear imaging was achieved, the shutter was opened at T1. Following a delay of 100 ms, the laser was initiated at T2, while a status monitor simultaneously

Fig. 6. Laser energy distribution under different path modes.
Fig. 7. X-ray detection of weld seams under different trajectory modes.
Fig. 8. X-ray real-time imaging of keyhole dynamic behavior under different trajectory modes.
Fig. 9. Dynamic behavior of keyholes under different trajectory patterns over multiple motion cycles.

Fig. 6(a)-6(d) illustrate the laser energy distributions under different trajectory modes. In the non-oscillation mode, the energy is continu­ ously distributed, with the highest energy density at the weld center and a gradual decrease with increasing distance from the center, consistent with a typical Gaussian profile. The peak energy density in this mode is 326.23 J/mm2. Compared with the non-oscillation mode, the circular trajectory exhibits a distinctly different energy distribution character­ ized by pronounced periodicity. From the side view, the distribution remains approximately Gaussian, with higher energy density at the center and lower values on both sides, yielding a central peak energy density of 483.50 J/mm2.For the infinity trajectory, the energy distri­ bution is similar to that of the circular trajectory and also shows periodic characteristics. Fig. 10. Comparison between simulation results and experimental results.

Fig. 8 illustrates the typical keyhole dynamic behavior within one oscillation cycle under different trajectory modes. Fig. 8(a) shows the variation in keyhole depth in the non-oscillation mode, where the minimum and maximum depths reach 0.87 mm and 2.07 mm, respec­ tively, exhibiting a random fluctuation pattern within a single cycle. Fig. 8(b) presents the keyhole dynamics under the circular trajectory mode. The keyhole depth exhibits a clear periodic variation, with larger depths observed at 225 ms and 275 ms compared with other times in the cycle, forming a characteristic “shallow–deep–shallow” pattern. As discussed in Section 3.1, trajectory superposition occurs at the weld
Fig. 11. Simulation result of CFD keyhole dynamic behavior.
Fig. 12. Schematic diagram of power modulation under different trajectory modes.

Fig. 9a shows the variation of keyhole depth under different trajec­ tory modes over multiple motion cycles. In the non-oscillation mode, the keyhole depth changes randomly. The maximum fluctuation reaches 2.27 mm. In the other three trajectory modes, the keyhole depth shows a periodic trend. The variation follows a regular pattern. The maximum fluctuation of keyhole depth is 1.01 mm for the circular trajectory, 0.81 mm for the infinite-shaped trajectory, and 1.17 mm for the figure-eight trajectory. Combined with the results in Section 3.1, different trajectory modes lead to different laser energy distribution patterns. These differ­ ences cause the periodic variation of keyhole depth. To further evaluate keyhole stability, the variance of keyhole depth over multiple motion cycles was calculated, as shown in Fig. 9c. A smaller value indicates higher stability. The non-oscillation mode shows the lowest stability.
Fig. 13. Laser energy distribution and weld seam X-ray detection results under power modulation mode.
Fig. 14. X-ray real-time imaging of keyhole dynamic behavior under different path power modulation mode.

Fig. 10 compares the simulated keyhole results with the in situ X-ray imaging experiments. The simulation shows good agreement with the experimental results. Fig. 11 presents the CFD simulation results of the keyhole dynamic behavior. During welding, the keyhole maintains a dynamic balance under the combined effects of recoil pressure, surface tension, and gravity. Recoil pressure plays a dominant role in sustaining the keyhole. It is strongly related to the input energy [31]. As shown in Fig. 11a, in the non-oscillation mode, the laser spot moves along the
Fig. 15. Keyhole dynamic behavior under different trajectory patterns over multiple motion cycles in power modulation mode.
Fig. 16. Simulation results of CFD keyhole dynamic behavior in the energy superposition region under two different power modes.

Fig. 13(a)-13(c) present the laser energy distribution and X-ray in­ spection results of the welds under power modulation. For the circular trajectory with power modulation, the energy retains a periodic distri­ bution; however, the side-view profile changes from the pre-modulation near-Gaussian distribution to a center-low, side-high pattern, with en­ ergy concentrated toward the sides. The peak energy densities on both sides are 507.86 J/mm2 and 526.27 J/mm2, while the central energy decreases to 469.32 J/mm2. In the infinity-shaped trajectory with power

tables.caption: Table 1 Chemical composition of titanium alloy.
Table 3 Calculation results of laser energy distribution.

Table 3 presents the calculated values of the transient line energy Epath under different trajectory modes within the same time period. The infinite-shaped trajectory shows the lowest Epathvalue. This result in­ dicates the strongest energy homogenization effect and the strongest stirring effect on the molten pool. The non-oscillation mode shows the highest Epath value. This mode exhibits the weakest energy homogeni­ zation effect. The degree of energy concentration is significantly higher than that of the trajectory modulation modes. The stirring effect on the molten pool is also the weakest in this mode.
